name: Smoking Salmon and Trout Part III - Smoking Fish
recipeInstructions:
1. Cold smoked products are still raw, deeply colored, with a texture like cured ham and can be thinly sliced without crumbling.
2. Hot smoked products are colored on the outside only and will flake like other styles of cooked fish.
3. Barbecued: smoke-cooked fish is made in a pre-heated covered barbecue or box-and-hotplate smoker; cooked in a smoky atmosphere without preliminary cold smoking or prior conditioning.
4. Kippered: fish are conditioned by first drying the fish in barely warm air, then bringing it up to cooking temperature gradually to improve appearance and quality.
5. Canning: fish is lightly smoked, high temperatures draw oil out to the surface; further dried before canning.
6. Cold smoked (Scotch/Irish/Norwegian/Nova Scotian): salted before smoking; still raw when finished but cured; appreciated by gourmets.
7. Lox: fresh fish lightly salted and mildly smoke-cured; must be refrigerated and is perishable.
8. Hard smoked: jerky-like, dehydrated; not refrigerated; traditionally prepared with fillets cut into thin strips, partially dried by wind or fan, then smoked for part of the drying time.
9. Pickle-smoked: fish are pickled before smoking to enhance taste; good for lean fish that smoke poorly.
10. Seelachs (ersatz salmon): salted, sliced thin, dyed and smoked white; easier to salt and smoke if fillet is chunked into pieces according to thickness.
11. Smoking Process:
- Filleting exposes more flesh to salt and smoke, allowing faster drying.
- Salt is necessary for flavor, releasing moisture, modifying flesh texture, and firming up the fish.
- Curing: draining brine and partially drying the fish; flavor develops fully (up to 24 hours).
- Smoking: done in forced draft units for uniform smoke exposure; natural draft smokers are unpredictable and may result in poor quality cooked fish.
12. Final preservation: refrigerate, freeze, or can promptly as smoked fish is still perishable except for hard-smoked varieties.
Extracted from: Smoking Salmon & Trout by Jack Whelan. Published by: Airie Publishing, Deep Bay, B.C. ISBN: 0-919807-00-3
